title: houston-unhoused
text:
This project is a project for the Media Innovation Group at the University of Texas at Austin in collaboration with Houston Public Media.
Analysis data takeaways:

title: Analysis
text:
Investigate citation numbers over time

What is the year with the most citations?
What is the month with the most citations?
What is the average number of citations for each month since 2016?

Investigate most common citation types

What are the most common citation types of all time?
What are the most common citation types by year?

Judgement and dispositions

What is the most common judgement?
What is the most common disposition?
How many guilty cases per year?
What is the month with the most guilty dispositions and highest percentage?
What is the average percentage of guilty dispositions for each month from 2016-2024?

Top people

Who has received the most citations? How many people received multiple citations/fines?
Individual fine statistics A. Who are the most heavily fined people? B. How much of those fines got dismissed? C. How much did they pay? D. How much do they still have due? E. Who has paid the highest amount of dues?
General fine statistics A. How many people still owe fines to the city? B. How many people have paid their fines? C. How many people have had their fines dismissed?

Fines

How many distinct people are there who were fined?
How many people still owe fines to the city? How much is the city owed in total?
How many people have paid some/all of their fines?
How many people have had some/all of their fines dismissed?

Jail time

How many jail judgments have been given?
How many individual people have been jailed? How many have been jailed more than once?

Individual people analysis

Kevin Shakesnider received the highest jail judgments, let’s visualize that.
Patricia Norris has the highest citation count, let’s visualize that.
Bobby Anderson
Rickey Garrett


To add perhaps: 5. Dismissal impacts i) Do dismissal rates change over time ii) How many people with multiple citations also had their fines dismissed? iii) Does time between citation date and judgment date impact dismissal? 6. Other i) How many individual people are there ii) Do people who receive time also receive fines?

title: Analysis
text:
1. Citation numbers over time

1.1. Year with most citations
What is the year with the most citations?

1.1.1 Creating distinct object
Right now, some rows have duplicate case numbers because the second appearance tracks how the case developed. So, to confidently know that each row does represent an individual citation, I will create an object that does not have duplicate rows. I will be keeping the second appearance because that provides the most updated data.

unhoused_distinct <- unhoused |> 
  group_by(case_number) |>  
  arrange(desc(judgment_date)) |> 
  slice(1) |>
  ungroup()

1.1.2 Finding year most citations

unhoused_distinct |> 
  filter(off_yr < 2025) |> 
  group_by(year(offense_date)) |> 
  summarize(yearly_offenses = n()) |> 
  arrange((yearly_offenses)) 


  





1.1.3 Data takeaways

The year with the highest number of citations is 2016 with 5800, followed by 2020 with 5011. The year with the lowest number of citations is 2018, at 1385.




1.2 Highest citation month
What’s the month with the most citations from 2016 to 2024?

unhoused_distinct |> 
  #filter(offense_date >= "2023-07-01") |> # i'm looking 6 months before Whitmire's term started to see if there is an uptick in citations after Jan. 2024.
  group_by(off_yr,
           off_mo_label) |> #@ get floor date
  summarise(
    yr_mo_off = sum(n())) |> 
  arrange(desc(yr_mo_off))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.



  




1.2.1 Data takeaways

Since 2016:

The month with the highest citation number since 2016 is Jan. 2021, with 797 citations. The month with the lowest citation number since 2016 is March 2022 with 29 citations.

Since July 2023, 6 months before Whitmire’s term began:

The month with the highest citation number since July 2023 is Sep. 2025 with 338 citations, followed by Aug. 2024 with 321 citations. The month with the lowest citation number since July 2023 is July 2023, with 66 citations. This is followed by May 2023 with 87 citations.





1.3 Avg monthly citation
What is the average monthly citation count for each month from 2016 to 2024?

unhoused_distinct |> 
  filter(off_yr < 2025) |> 
  group_by(off_yr,
           off_mo_label) |> 
  summarise(
    yr_mo_off = n()
  ) |> 
  group_by(off_mo_label) |> 
  summarise(avg_mo_off = round(mean(yr_mo_off), 1)) |> 
  arrange(desc(avg_mo_off))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.



  




1.3.1 Data takeaways

Considering every month from 2016 to 2024, the month with the highest average monthly citation count is January, with about 326 average monthly offenses, followed by May with about 307. The month with the lowest is November, with about 192 average monthly offenses, followed by December with about 201.

title: Analysis
text:
2. Citation types

2.1 Most common
Finding the most common violation types.

unhoused_distinct |> 
  group_by(violation_code, violation_description) |> 
  summarise(violations = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(violations))

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'violation_code'. You can override using
the `.groups` argument.



  




2.1.1 Data takeaways
There are seven types of violations that have been given since 2016. Here is what they mean and their frequency:

CC753 has 21 citations Street vendor (separate)

Selling goods in public area: STREET VENDOR (EXPOSE FOR SALE) (SELL) GOODS, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (PROPERTY)

CC921 has 477 citations Sidewalk

Sidewalk obstruction: OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BY (PLACING/DEPOSITING/PERMIT PERSON UNDER CONTROL) OBJECT (BOX/MAT./VEH./ETC.)

CC940 has 15,038 citations Civility

Sidewalk obstruction: SIT/LIE DOWN ON (BLANKET/STOOL) PLACED ON SIDEWALK B/W 7:00 AM AND 11:00 PM IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC941 has 13,591 citations Civility

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE (BED MAT./PERSONAL POSS.) ON SIDEWALK B/W THE HRS OF 7:00 A.M. & 11:00P.M. IN A PROHIBITED AREA

CC942 has 97 citations Sidewalk

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK WITHOUT A PERMIT

CC943 has 5 citations Sidewalk

Sidewalk obstruction: IMPAIR OR OBSTRUCT SIDEWALK BEYOND SCOPE OF PERMIT

CC948 has 229 citations Sidewalk

Sidewalk obstruction: PLACE OR ALLOW OBSTRUCTION ON SIDEWALK

CC956 has 1 citation Separate

EXPOSE FOR SALE MERCHANDISE, TO-WIT:…ON A PUBLIC (SIDEWALK) (STREET) (ESPLANADE) (PROPERTY)


The most common citation since 2016 is CC940 with 15,038 offenses, with CC941 as a runner-up at 13,591 offenses. The next most common is CC291 with only 477 offenses.



2.2 Most common by year

unhoused_distinct |> 
  group_by(off_yr,
           violation_code)|> 
  summarise(violations = n()) |> 
  arrange(desc(off_yr)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = off_yr, y = violations, color = violation_code)) +
  geom_line()

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'off_yr'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.










2.2.1 Data takeaways

All 21 of the CC753 citations, all 97 of CC942 citations, all five of CC943 citations and all 229 of CC948 citations have been given in 2025.

title: Analysis
Analysis
text:
4. Top people

4.1 Distinct people
How many distinct people have received a citation?

unhoused_distinct |> 
  summarise(people_count = n_distinct(refined_name))


  




4.1.1 Data takeaway

There are 4598 distinct people who have received a citation since 2016.




4.2 Most citations
Who has received the most citations?

unhoused_distinct |> 
  group_by(refined_name) |> 
  summarise(citation_count = n(),
            first_citation = min(offense_date),
            last_citation = max(offense_date),
            ) |> 
  arrange(desc(citation_count)) |> 
  filter(citation_count >= 100)


  




4.2.1 Data takeaways

There are 26 people who have received at least 100 total citations.
The person with the most citations is Patricia Norris, who has received 794 total citations, all given between October 2017 and May 2025.
The person with the second most citations is Forest H Smith, who has received 336 total citations, all given between August 2020 and October 2025




4.3 Most fines and dues
Who are the most heavily fined people? How much of those fines got dismissed? How much did they pay? How much do they still have due? Who has paid the highest amount of dues?

individual_fines <- unhoused_distinct |>
  group_by(refined_name) |>
  summarise(total_fined = sum(amount_fined, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_dismissed = sum(fines_dismissed, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_paid = sum(fines_paid, na.rm = TRUE),
    total_fines_due = sum(fines_due, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(total_fines_paid))

individual_fines


  




4.3.1 Data takeaways

Patricia Norris has been charged the highest total fines at around $199,123. She hasn’t paid any of her fines, but has had about $149,118 dismissed, which leaves about $50,006 that she still owes.
Forest H Smith is the second most fined person, with around $83,238 total fines. He has also never paid any of his fines, but has had roughly $9,584 dismissed, leaving around $73,654 that he still owes.
Kevin P Shakesnider is the person who has paid the highest amount of fines. He has paid about $28,684 of his total $44,453, and has had roughly $10,438 dismissed. As of October 2025, he still owes about $5,331.

title: Analysis
Analysis
Analysis
text:
7. Individual people analysis

7.1. Kevin P Shakesnider
Shakesnider data takeaways refresher: * Highest fines: Kevin P Shakesnider is the person who has paid the highest amount of fines. He has paid about $28,684 of his total $44,453, and has had roughly $10,438 dismissed. As of October 2025, he still owes about $5,331. * Highest “CREDIT TIME SERVED” judgments: The person with the highest credit_jail_judgments is Kevin P Shakesnider, who has received 136 individual “CREDIT TIME SERVED” judgments. Those 136 judgments were given across 22 distinct judgment dates.

7.1.1 Highest “CREDIT TIME SERVED” judgments
When did Kevin P Shakesnider receive the highest jail citations in one judgment? What is the overall date range?

unhoused_distinct |> 
  filter(refined_name == "SHAKESNIDER, KEVIN P") |>
  group_by(judgment_date, violation_code) |>
 summarise(jail_count = n())

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'judgment_date'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.



  




7.1.1.1 Data takeaways
All 21 of his judgment dates fall between Jan. 1 and Dec. 22 2016. On June 23, he received a “CREDIT TIME SERVED” judgment for 16 citations, his highest in one single day.



7.1.2 Plotting
Plotting citations and violation type.

7.1.2.1 Preparing object

shakesnider_jailed <- unhoused |> 
  filter(refined_name == "SHAKESNIDER, KEVIN P") |>
  group_by(judgment_date, violation_code) |>
 summarise(jail_count = n())

`summarise()` has grouped output by 'judgment_date'. You can override using the
`.groups` argument.

shakesnider_jailed


  





7.1.2.2 Plotting

ggplot(shakesnider_jailed, 
       aes(x = judgment_date, y = jail_count, fill = violation_code)) +
  geom_col(position = "stack") +
  labs(
    title = "Kevin P Shakesnider's Jail Judgments Over Time",
    x = "Judgment Date",
    y = "Number of Citations",

7.2 Patricia Norris

7.2.1 Highest citation count
Making Norris object.

norris <- unhoused |> 
  filter(refined_name == "NORRIS, PATRICIA")

norris


  





7.2.2 Plotting

Checking for what kinds of violation codes Norris has been given.

norris |> 
  distinct(violation_code)


  



Making the object.

norris_citations <- norris |> 
  group_by(offense_date) |> 
  summarise(citation_count = n()) |>
  arrange(offense_date)

norris_citations


  




7.2.2.1.1 Data takeaways

Each one of these points is a day, so the higher the citation count, the more citations she received in a single day. Almost every time Patricia Norris has been given a citation, she has also been given at least one other citation. She was also hit with citations almost consistently from 2020 to present. There have been some gaps between those dates, I wonder what those dates are.

title: Analysis
text:
8. Geocode mapping

8.1 Importing district data
Importing Houston district data as an sf obejct.

hou_districts <- st_read(
  "https://mycity2.houstontx.gov/pubgis02/rest/services/HoustonMap/Administrative_Boundary/MapServer/2/query?where=1=1&outFields=*&f=geojson"
)

Reading layer `OGRGeoJSON' from data source

8.4 Fixing invalid district lines

districts <- st_make_valid(districts) # this function goes through every polygon to fix problems like duplicate points and self intersections, which i was having trouble with.


districts <- st_transform(districts, 4326)



8.4 Citation to district
Assigning each citation to a district.

districting_citations <- st_join(pts, districts, join = st_within)

districting_citations


  





8.5 Preparing to plot
Grouping data by district, preparing to map.

district_counts <- districting_citations |>
  st_drop_geometry() |> # this removes the geometry so we can summarize citation points
  group_by(DISTRICT) |>
  summarize(citations = n())

district_counts


  



Adding a new column for citations to each district polygon.

unhoused_mapped <- districts |>
  left_join(district_counts, by = "DISTRICT")

unhoused_mapped


  





8.6 Plotting

ggplot(unhoused_mapped) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = citations), color = "white", size = 0.3) +
  geom_sf_text(aes(label = DISTRICT), size = 4, color = "black") +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "plasma", na.value = "grey90") +
  labs(
    title = "Citations Given to unhoused people by Houston City Council district",
    caption = "By Layla Dajani",
    fill = "Citations",
    x = "Longitude",
    y = "Latitude"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

5. Duplicate rows

5.1 Finding dupes

unhoused_selected |> 
   get_dupes(data_origin)


  





5.2 Removing dupes

unhoused_to_refine <- unhoused_selected |>
  arrange(desc(data_origin)) |> # distinct() keeps the first row between two duplicates, so arranging it in descending order by data_origin, it makes sure we keep the most recent row
  distinct(across(!c(
    data_origin, 
    defendant_address)),
    .keep_all = TRUE)

title: Cleaning
text:
7. Creating and selecting columns

7.1 Whitmire flag
I chose to separate these columns because there may be cases where a citation was given before Whitmire’s term began, but didn’t receive a judgment until after.

7.1.2 Offense date
Creating a column indicating whether an offense occurred before or after Whitmire’s term began.

unhoused_whitmire_offense <- unhoused_refined |>
  mutate(
    whitmire_offense = if_else( #if_else function creates a new column based on its argument. it instructs that if the argument is true, use "before," if it's false, use "after." this makes sure we include Jan. 1 2024 in "after"
      offense_date < as.Date("2024-01-01"),
      "before",
      "after"
    )
  )
